summary: I am Linda Gorton, currently serving my second term as the mayor of Lexington, Kentucky. I have a background as a registered nurse, which has greatly influenced how I approach leadership—focusing on assessment, planning, and action just like in nursing. Leading the city through the 250Lex year-long celebration has been a tremendous honor and responsibility, and I’ve enjoyed empowering others to take leadership roles in this collaborative effort. My goal has always been to serve the people of Lexington with transparency, ethics, and care.

Lexington is my home and the city I am privileged to lead as mayor. In 2023 and 2024, we celebrated 250 years of Lexington’s history with a year-long event called 250Lex, which highlighted everything from equine culture to literature and sports. Lexington is more than just horse racing and bourbon; it’s a vibrant city with a rich cultural and historical tapestry that we wanted to showcase. I’ve seen firsthand how this city has grown and changed, and it’s been exciting to bring people together to celebrate its diverse heritage.

250Lex was a year-long celebration we organized to mark Lexington’s 250th anniversary, starting planning about two years ago. Unlike other southern cities that had only a weekend celebration, we chose a full year to highlight different themes each month, like Music Month, Culinary Month, and Sports Month. It was a huge collaborative effort involving a commission, co-chairs, and ambassadors who helped plan and execute events that engaged thousands of people. This celebration allowed us to recognize both the good and complex parts of our history while looking forward to the future.

Kentucky is the state I serve as mayor of one of its key cities, Lexington. It’s a place with a deep and sometimes complicated history, which we acknowledged during the 250Lex celebrations. Kentucky is known for many things, including its literary contributions; several Poets Laureate of Kentucky have come from Lexington. I’m proud to represent Kentucky’s values of community, history, and progress in my role.

Lexington has been home to several Poets Laureate of Kentucky, which is something we highlighted during the literature month of 250Lex. It’s a testament to the city’s rich literary culture and the talent that has come from here. Celebrating these poets helped us show a side of Lexington that many people might not immediately think of, beyond the usual associations with horses and bourbon.

Kenny Brooks is the women’s basketball coach in Lexington, and he played a key role during Sports Month of the 250Lex celebration. We invited him along with other coaches to participate in basketball week, which was part of our effort to highlight the city’s sports culture. It was great to bring attention to local sports figures who contribute so much to the community’s identity and pride.

Mark Pope is the men’s basketball coach here in Lexington, and he was involved in the Sports Month activities during 250Lex. Alongside Kenny Brooks and local high school coaches, he helped us celebrate basketball week, which was a highlight for many sports fans in the city. His participation helped showcase the strength of Lexington’s sports programs and community spirit.

Before the COVID-19 pandemic hit, I had already advocated for hand sanitizer on conference tables to reduce the spread of germs, which some people thought was unusual at the time. When the pandemic arrived, those sanitary practices proved to be very important. My background as a nurse made me more aware of health and safety measures, and that experience helped me lead the city with a focus on public health during the crisis.
